extractedText:
Discovery Meeting Call Guide  —  March 31, 2026  —  In-Person @ Ivanhoe Village



MEETING FLOW  —  TARGET 60–90 MIN

Time

Section

Focus

10 min

Warm Up

Personal catch-up. Let Charlie set context. Acknowledge the history.

15 min

BlueWave Today

How the business runs now. Org structure, call flow, pain points. Let him talk.

15 min

The Vision

What does Charlie want to build? Ideal state in his own words. Build vs. buy thinking. Exit strategy angle.

10 min

Systems & Tech

Bullhorn, RingCentral, anything else? Who manages them? Existing automations? Data quality?

10 min

Scope & Priorities

What to build first? Internal tool vs. product? Call triage vs. other channels?

10 min

Timeline & Budget

Target timeline for v1? Who approves? Budget allocated or exploratory?

10 min

Next Steps

Summarize. Flag gaps. Explain our process: intake → ROM. Set follow-up timeline.

DISCOVERY QUESTIONS  —  AIM FOR 10–12 TOTAL

Glance, don’t read. Follow the conversation. Check off as answered.

#

QUESTION

SIGNAL TO CAPTURE

ASKED

ABOUT THE OUTCOME  (ask at least 3)

1

When this AI system is working, what does a typical day look like for your team vs. today?

Operational vision

□

2

Is this tool something you’d license to other staffing firms, or purely internal to BlueWave?

Product vs. internal tool

□

3

What metrics would tell you this investment paid off? Placement speed, call resolution, recruiter hours saved?

Value in numbers

□

4

If call triage works perfectly, what does it unlock next? Website, shared inbox, bigger product vision?

Full roadmap

□

ABOUT THE URGENCY  (ask at least 2)

5

What’s driving the timing? Is the exit timeline creating the deadline, or is there an operational pain getting worse?

Strategic vs. operational

□

6

Walk me through what happens when someone calls (407) 986-5777 right now.

Current reality

□

7

Has call volume or complexity grown recently? What changed?

Scaling vs. efficiency

□

ABOUT THE COST OF INACTION  (ask at least 1)

8

What does it cost BlueWave when a call gets misrouted, or a candidate checks interview status and nobody has an answer?

Pain in dollars

□

9

How many calls/week come into the main number? How many get handled well vs. dropped?

Volume data

□

ABOUT THE CLIENT’S SITUATION  (ask at least 3)

10

Who’s the day-to-day point of contact and decision-maker? Is that Laura, or someone else?

Real stakeholder

□

11

What’s the full tech stack beyond Bullhorn and RingCentral?

Hidden dependencies

□

12

Does your team have technical staff — someone managing Bullhorn config or IT?

Hand-holding level

□

13

Have you worked with a dev shop or agency on custom software before?

Engagement expectations

□

14

Who else weighs in on budget and scope — Michael, Laura, full leadership?

Decision structure

□



SIGNALS TO WATCH

✅  GREEN FLAGS

• Charlie articulates outcome in concrete terms

• Real external timeline driver (exit in ~2 years)

• Budget capacity exists (~$40M firm, exit-motivated)

• Decision-maker in the room with strategic motivation

• Aligns with MF core: voice AI, LLM, API orchestration

⚠️  YELLOW FLAGS

• Already decided what to build, just wants a price

• Scope expands with every question asked

• Laura/execs seem resistant or uninformed

• Expects full product in 6 months (timeline mismatch)

• “Build us a SaaS product” = very different scope

COMPETITIVE QUICK REFERENCE  —  KNOW BEFORE YOU GO

TOOL

WHAT IT DOES

WHY IT’S NOT ENOUGH

Bullhorn Amplify

AI matching, summaries, outreach drafting inside Bullhorn. 51% more submissions.

No voice AI, no call handling, no inbound triage. Recruiter productivity only.

RingCentral AIR

24/7 AI call answering, intent routing, lead capture, SMS follow-up. OpenAI-powered.

No Bullhorn integration. Data locked inside RC. Can’t export via API. No IP ownership.

Tenzo AI

AI interviewing with deep Bullhorn integration. Writes scores/notes to candidate records.

Outbound screening only. Not inbound call triage. Doesn’t handle clients/employees/contractors.

Whippy / Brilo

AI candidate engagement, pre-screening, SMS. Bullhorn integrations available.

Point solutions for candidate pipeline. Not unified multi-caller-type triage.

The gap: No existing product handles inbound triage across clients, employees, contractors, AND candidates through a single system that writes back to Bullhorn. That’s the custom build lane.

DATA POINTS TO CAPTURE  —  WRITE ANSWERS IN THE MEETING

DATA POINT

NOTES

Desired outcome (Charlie’s own words)

 

Value of outcome ($ terms, even rough)

 

Urgency driver (exit, operational, or both)

 

Cost of inaction (monthly cost of status quo)

 

Call volume (calls/day or week to main number)

 

Current call process (who answers, what breaks)

 

Internal tool vs. licensable product

 

Full tech stack (beyond Bullhorn + RC)

 

Technical staff on their side?

 

Budget range or capacity signals

 

Decision-making structure / who approves

 

Timeline expectation for first working version

 

BEFORE YOU LEAVE THE ROOM

1. Summarize what you heard back to Charlie. 

2. Flag any gaps needing a second discovery session (especially if ops team wasn’t present). 

3. Set the expectation: “We’ll come back with a rough scope and estimate within [X] days.” 

4. Debrief as a team immediately after (parking lot is fine).

title: Development Scope
extractedText:
BlueWave Resource Partners x Mirror Factory 

Project Swell Product Scope Document



Prepared by: Mirror Factory

Prepared for: BlueWave Leadership Team

Date: April 5, 2026

Revision: 0.2 (Draft)





1. Executive Summary

The Problem

BlueWave's recruiters spend 75–80% of their time on unqualified candidates. When a new job requisition arrives (e.g., SpaceX needs 30 electrical engineers in two weeks), it takes days of manual sorting through hundreds of applicants before a recruiter even makes a call. Inbound calls (~8–10/day) consume admin and recruiter time on low-value tasks like password resets and status checks. BlueWave evaluated existing solutions (RingCentral AIR, Bullhorn Amplify, Tenzo AI) and found none that solve the full problem.

The Solution

Project Swell is a proprietary AI system with two core components:

Inbound Call Triage (MVP) — AI receptionist answers calls, determines intent, and routes to Sales, Admin/HR, or Candidates. Candidate calls transition to a recruiter agent for pre-screening. Every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries.

Outbound Candidate Screening (Parallel Development) — On receipt of a new job req, the system screens hundreds of candidates against qualifying criteria and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour.

Architecture

Two-layer model with separated IP ownership:

Layer

Owner

Contains

Swell (Orchestration)

BlueWave

Routing logic, ticketing, staffing integrations, screening rubrics, messaging bus

Layers (Platform)

Mirror Factory

Context management, agent profiles, rules, integration framework, authoring tools



Swell functions independently of Layers. BlueWave owns their product; Mirror Factory retains and evolves its platform.



Phased Delivery

Phase

Duration

Focus

Design & Development

4–6 weeks

Build MVP (inbound triage), begin outbound screening architecture

Pilot

~90 days

Internal pilot with progressive delegation, continuous QA, outbound development continues

Expansion

TBD

Activate outbound screening, evaluate productization, design additional tools



Key Outcomes

Outcome

Current State

Target State

Recruiter capacity

~1,000 candidates at capacity

5,000–10,000 with AI assistance

Time-to-qualified-shortlist

Days of manual sorting

Under 1 hour

Low-value call handling

Manual triage, 8–10 calls/day

AI-resolved or intelligently routed

Software IP

None — using third-party SaaS

Proprietary asset on balance sheet





2. Product Vision & Objectives

Strategic Context

BlueWave is a bootstrapped, revenue-funded staffing firm that has grown from sub-$1M in 2014 to approximately $40M annually. Charlie Lewis intends to exit the business within two years. Owning proprietary software IP — not licensing third-party tools — is a strategic lever to maximize the exit valuation multiple. Every architecture and scope decision in this engagement should be evaluated against this exit-readiness lens.

No existing product handles the full problem: multi-caller-type inbound triage with deep Bullhorn integration, combined with outbound screening at scale. This gap validates the custom-build thesis.



Jobs to Be Done

These JTBD statements capture what BlueWave is hiring this system to accomplish, derived directly from the March 31 discovery meeting. Each statement connects to specific features in Section 5.

When a call comes into BlueWave's main number, I want the caller's intent identified and routed to the right person automatically, so that recruiters and admins only handle calls requiring human judgment. (→ AI Receptionist, §5.1.1)

When we receive a new job requisition, I want hundreds of candidates screened against the real qualifying criteria within the first hour, so that my recruiters start calling qualified, interested people instead of sorting through databases for days. (→ Outbound Screening, §5.2)

When a candidate calls in, I want them pre-screened by an AI recruiter that captures structured evaluation data and writes it to Bullhorn, so that the human recruiter who follows up already knows if this person is a fit. (→ Recruiter Agent, §5.1.5)

When someone has called multiple times about the same issue, I want all of that history merged into a single context-rich ticket, so that whoever picks up the next call has full context and the caller doesn't have to repeat themselves. (→ Native Ticketing, §5.3)

When an AI agent handles a call or screens a candidate, I want a full action trace I can audit weekly, so that I can catch mistakes, improve the system, and build trust progressively. (→ Audit Interface, §5.5)

Core Objectives

Eliminate low-value inbound call handling. Route calls intelligently so recruiters, admins, and sales staff only handle calls that require human judgment. Resolve self-service tasks (e.g., ADP password resets, status checks) without human intervention.

Increase recruiter capacity 5–10x. Move from approximately 1,000 candidates per recruiter at capacity to intelligently managing 5,000–10,000 through AI-assisted screening and triage.

Compress time-to-qualified-shortlist. Reduce the cycle from receiving a new job requisition to having a qualified, contacted candidate shortlist from days of manual sorting and calling to under one hour.

Create standalone software IP. Architect Swell as a separable asset on the balance sheet — owned entirely by BlueWave — that increases company valuation at exit.

Build a native ticketing and context system. Every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries tailored to the recipient, enabling warm handoffs to humans with full context.

Design Philosophy

Charlie emphasized a core principle during discovery: get humans talking to the right people faster, not replace humans. BlueWave is a relationship-driven business. AI augments recruiter capacity and decision quality — it does not remove the human element. The system should be modeled like managing a team of interns: given clear instructions, deployed in a controlled environment, audited regularly, and improved through feedback loops.



3. System Architecture

Two-Layer Model

Project Swell is built on a two-layer architecture that separates client-specific orchestration from reusable platform infrastructure. This separation serves two purposes: it gives BlueWave clean IP ownership of their business logic, and it gives Mirror Factory a reusable platform foundation for future engagements.

Swell — Orchestration Layer (BlueWave IP)

Swell is the orchestration layer that sits on top of Layers. It contains all business logic, routing decisions, and staffing-specific functionality. BlueWave owns this IP outright.

Swell encompasses:

Call routing logic and decision engine (the rules governing which calls go where, under what conditions)

Native ticketing system (progressive ticket creation, caller ID merging, contextual summaries)

Staffing-specific integrations (Bullhorn ATS/CRM read/write, RingCentral telephony)

Human/agent coordination workflows (escalation paths, handoff protocols, notification routing)

Recruiter agent screening logic and rubrics (qualifying criteria, evaluation scoring, structured output)

Internal messaging bus (SOP-driven routing across text, Slack, and email)

Audit trail and reporting interface (agent action trace, weekly review tooling, error flagging)

Layers — Platform Layer (Mirror Factory IP)

Layers provides the context management infrastructure that Swell runs on. It is not required for Swell to function — Swell can operate with generic markdown-based context — but Layers provides best-practice scaffolding that makes the system faster to build, easier to maintain, and higher quality.

Layers encompasses:

Context management infrastructure (how agent context is structured, stored, and retrieved)

Agent profiles, rules, and skills definitions (the framework for defining what agents know and can do)

Integration framework and connectors (standardized patterns for connecting to external systems)

Authoring tools (lightweight interfaces for viewing and editing context — comparable to Obsidian for knowledge management)

Best practices for context structuring (analogous to Unity over raw DirectX — faster to build on, not strictly required)

Layers is portable and reusable. Any custom authoring tools developed during this engagement remain in Layers and carry forward to future Mirror Factory clients.

Relationship Between Layers

Swell sits on top of Layers but is not locked in. BlueWave can unplug Layers and substitute generic markdown — the system still functions, just without the best-practice scaffolding. This ensures BlueWave's IP independence while allowing Mirror Factory to retain and evolve its platform.

Deployment Considerations

Deployment model is TBD and will be resolved during the Design & Development phase. Options discussed:

Cloud (e.g., AWS Bedrock): Enterprise-grade infrastructure, managed scaling. Not fully air-gapped — tokens still touch external infrastructure. Security implications need evaluation.

Local models (e.g., Gemma 26B+): Full data privacy, no external data exposure. Viable if the testing and validation pipeline is strong enough. Higher operational overhead.

Hybrid: Cloud for non-sensitive operations, local for candidate data processing. Balances security with operational simplicity.

The deployment decision affects security posture, operating costs, voice model selection, and the QA approach. BlueWave's requirement is a private, closed-loop deployment with no external data exposure.

Implementation Stack

Implementation approach will be finalized during Design & Development. Candidates discussed:

Python-based orchestrator (similar to existing Mirror Factory patterns)

Next.js modeled on open-source agent frameworks (OpenAI/Anthropic patterns)

Voice model TBD: cloud options (ElevenLabs, AWS Bedrock) vs. local models

Standards for building orchestrators to be established during this engagement



4. Agent Architecture

This section defines the AI agent system that powers Swell's core functionality. Agent architecture decisions made here affect scope, feasibility, and the testing strategy.

Agent Roster

Agent

Role

Activation Trigger

Phase

Receptionist

Answers inbound calls, gathers intent, routes to the correct path

Any inbound call to BlueWave's main number

MVP

Recruiter

Conducts pre-screening conversations, evaluates candidates against a rubric, produces structured output

Candidate call identified by Receptionist

MVP

Screening Agent

Initiates outbound contact to candidates, evaluates against job req criteria, compiles shortlists

New job requisition received in Bullhorn

Parallel Dev



Agent Design Principles

Single responsibility per agent. Each agent has one clear job. The Receptionist routes; it does not screen. The Recruiter screens; it does not route.

Progressive delegation. Agents start with low-stakes tasks and expand responsibility as confidence builds during the Pilot. The system begins with simple routing (Admin/status checks) before handling candidate screening calls.

Human escalation as default. When an agent encounters uncertainty, the default is to escalate to a human with full context — not to guess. The threshold for escalation will be tuned during the Pilot.

Auditable action trace. Every agent decision is logged: what data was accessed, what routing decision was made, what output was generated. This trace feeds the audit interface and enables the weekly review workflow.

Inter-Agent Communication

The Receptionist and Recruiter agents operate within the same call. When the Receptionist identifies a candidate, it performs a warm handoff to the Recruiter agent — the caller experiences a seamless transition, not a transfer. Context gathered by the Receptionist (caller name, intent, any Bullhorn match) is passed to the Recruiter agent so the screening conversation starts with context, not from scratch.

The Screening Agent (outbound) operates independently of the inbound agents. It is triggered by Bullhorn events (new requisition), not by calls.



Agent Context Management

Each agent's behavior is governed by its context profile within Layers:

System prompt architecture — defines the agent's role, personality, boundaries, and escalation rules

Tool definitions — what external systems the agent can access and what operations it can perform (e.g., Recruiter agent can read Bullhorn candidate records and write evaluation data)

Guardrails — explicit boundaries on what the agent should never do (e.g., never promise a candidate they have the job, never share salary information that hasn't been authorized)

Versioning — context profiles are versioned so changes can be rolled back if a new version underperforms



5. Feature Specifications

Features are organized by component. Each feature includes a description, acceptance criteria, phase designation, and priority (Must/Should/Could per MoSCoW). AI-specific features include a hypothesis statement with validation criteria.

5.1 Inbound Call Triage (MVP)

5.1.1 AI Receptionist Agent

Priority: Must

Description: An AI agent that answers inbound calls to BlueWave's main number, introduces itself, gathers caller intent through natural conversation, and routes the call to the appropriate department or agent based on configurable rules.

Hypothesis: We believe an LLM-powered voice agent can accurately classify caller intent into one of three routing paths (Sales, Admin/HR, Candidates) with 90%+ accuracy given a structured greeting and 2–3 clarifying questions. This will be validated during the Pilot phase with a target of [TBD] live calls.

Acceptance Criteria:

Agent answers inbound calls within 2 rings and delivers a natural, branded greeting

Agent determines caller intent through open-ended conversation (not menu trees)

Agent routes calls to one of three primary paths: Sales, Admin/HR, or Candidates

Agent resolves self-service requests without human escalation where possible (e.g., ADP password reset instructions, basic status checks)

Agent creates a ticket for every call (see §5.3 Native Ticketing)

Routing rules are configurable without code changes

Agent gracefully handles edge cases: unclear intent, caller frustration, requests to speak to a human immediately

5.1.2 Sales Path Routing

Priority: Must

Description: Inbound calls identified as sales-related (prospective clients, business development inquiries, partnership requests) are routed to the appropriate sales team member or voicemail.

Acceptance Criteria:

Calls identified as sales/business development are routed per BlueWave's Sales routing SOPs

Caller context (name, company, stated need) is captured and included in the handoff ticket

If no sales team member is available, the agent captures a message and notifies the appropriate person via the internal messaging bus

Dependency: Sales routing SOPs must be documented by BlueWave (see §14)

5.1.3 Admin/HR Path Routing

Priority: Must

Description: Inbound calls related to benefits questions, ADP issues, internal department routing, and general administrative inquiries are routed to the Admin/HR team or resolved by the agent directly.

Acceptance Criteria:

Agent identifies admin/HR intent and routes per BlueWave's Admin routing SOPs

Common self-service tasks are handled by the agent without escalation (e.g., ADP password reset instructions, benefits information from a knowledge base)

Escalation paths are defined for issues requiring human judgment

Caller context and resolution status are captured in the ticket

Dependency: Admin routing SOPs and self-service knowledge base content must be provided by BlueWave (see §14)

5.1.4 Candidate Path Routing

Priority: Must

Description: Inbound calls from candidates (new applicants and existing candidates checking status, scheduling interviews, or inquiring about positions) are identified, classified as new or existing, and routed accordingly.

Acceptance Criteria:

Agent distinguishes between new candidates and existing candidates (via caller ID, name match against Bullhorn, or stated intent)

New candidates are routed to the Recruiter Agent for pre-screening (see §5.1.5)

Existing candidates checking status receive automated updates where data is available in Bullhorn, with escalation to a human recruiter if the query requires judgment

All candidate interactions are logged to Bullhorn

5.1.5 Recruiter Agent (Pre-Screening)

Priority: Must

Description: When a candidate call is identified, the system transitions from the Receptionist Agent to a Recruiter Agent that leads a structured pre-screening conversation. The Recruiter Agent evaluates the candidate against a rubric, captures structured data, and produces an evaluation summary.

Hypothesis: We believe an AI agent can lead a structured pre-screening conversation that captures enough structured data (3–5 qualifying criteria evaluated, candidate availability, compensation expectations) to produce a recruiter-ready assessment that accelerates — not replaces — the human recruiter's follow-up decision. Validation: during Pilot, compare agent-screened candidates against recruiter-screened candidates for assessment agreement rate.

Acceptance Criteria:

Recruiter Agent leads the conversation from a pre-screening perspective (agent drives, candidate responds)

Agent evaluates candidate against 3–5 qualifying criteria defined per job type or general screening rubric

Structured output is captured: candidate profile, evaluation scores against criteria, call summary, and recruiter-ready assessment

Output is written to Bullhorn (candidate record, tear sheet, and/or notes)

If the candidate is a strong match for an active requisition, the ticket is flagged for immediate recruiter follow-up

Conversation feels natural — not like a survey or checklist

Dependency: BlueWave must define the 3–5 most common qualifying criteria used across job types (see §14)

5.2 Outbound Candidate Screening (Parallel Development)

5.2.1 Requisition Ingestion

Priority: Must (for outbound component)

Description: When BlueWave receives a new job requisition, the system ingests the job description, identifies the actual qualifying criteria (stripping out boilerplate), and prepares a screening profile.

Hypothesis: We believe an LLM can reliably extract the 3–5 critical qualifying criteria from a job description, separating them from standard boilerplate, with sufficient accuracy that recruiters only need to review and adjust rather than define from scratch. Validation: compare agent-extracted criteria against recruiter-defined criteria across 20+ historical job requisitions.

Acceptance Criteria:

System accepts job requisitions from Bullhorn (automated) or manual input

System parses the job description and extracts the 3–5 critical qualifying criteria

Screening profile is generated and available for review before outbound screening begins

Qualifying criteria can be manually adjusted by a recruiter before screening launch

5.2.2 Candidate Matching and Outreach

Priority: Must (for outbound component)

Description: The system searches BlueWave's Bullhorn database (~200K candidates), identifies potential matches against the screening profile, and initiates outreach to qualified candidates simultaneously via call, text, and/or email.

Acceptance Criteria:

System searches Bullhorn candidate database against screening profile criteria

Outreach is initiated to matched candidates simultaneously (target: hundreds per requisition)

Outreach channels include phone, text, and email (channel selection based on candidate data availability and BlueWave preferences)

Candidate responses are captured, evaluated against the screening rubric, and scored

Qualified, interested candidates are compiled into a shortlist and pushed to a Bullhorn tear sheet

Target: qualified shortlist delivered within 1 hour of requisition receipt

Slack notification sent to recruiting team (e.g., "45 screened electrical engineers added to tear sheet for Job #142")

Dependency: Bullhorn API read/write confirmation (see §14). Per-click sourcing tools excluded until cost controls are established.

5.2.3 Screening Agent Learning

Priority: Could (Expansion phase)

Description: The outbound screening agent improves over time by learning from response rates, bounce rates, screening question effectiveness, and placement outcomes.

Acceptance Criteria:

System tracks response rates per outreach channel and candidate segment

System tracks which screening questions best predict candidate quality

Learning insights are surfaced in the audit interface for recruiter review

Agent behavior can be adjusted based on accumulated learning without code changes

5.3 Native Ticketing System (MVP)

5.3.1 Ticket Creation and Progressive Fill

Priority: Must

Description: Every inbound call immediately generates a blank ticket that fills progressively as the call unfolds. The ticket is not a raw transcript — it is a structured, contextual record of the interaction.

Acceptance Criteria:

Blank ticket is created the instant a call connects, before the agent speaks

Ticket captures: caller ID, timestamp, intent classification, routing decision, conversation summary, evaluation data (if applicable), and resolution status

Ticket fills progressively during the call — information is added as it becomes available, not retroactively after the call ends

Ticket includes full action trace (every agent decision is logged and traceable)

5.3.2 Repeat Caller Merging

Priority: Must

Description: When the same person calls again (identified by caller ID, name, or other matching criteria), tickets are automatically merged to create a compound interaction history.

Acceptance Criteria:

System identifies repeat callers via phone number, name match, or Bullhorn candidate ID

Repeat caller tickets are merged into a single interaction history

Prior context surfaces automatically when a repeat caller is identified (e.g., "This is Fraser's 6th call about the Disney electrical engineer position")

Merged ticket history is accessible to both agents and humans

5.3.3 Contextual Handoff Summaries

Priority: Must

Description: When a call must be routed to a human, the ticket provides a contextual summary tailored to the specific recipient — not a generic transcript dump.

Acceptance Criteria:

Ticket summary is generated in a format appropriate to the recipient's role:

Charlie receives escalation context (e.g., "Fraser has called 16 times — recommend a direct call, he's escalating")

Recruiters receive candidate-fit summaries (e.g., "Strong match for Job #142 — 4/5 criteria met, available immediately, requesting $85/hr")

Admin receives resolution context (e.g., "ADP password reset — caller attempted self-service, needs manual intervention")

Human recipient receives the summary via the internal messaging bus before the call is transferred (where possible) or immediately after

No re-explanation is required — the human has full context from the ticket

5.4 Internal Messaging Bus (MVP)

5.4.1 SOP-Driven Message Routing

Priority: Must

Description: A central routing layer that understands BlueWave's internal communication SOPs and delivers agent notifications, ticket summaries, and escalation alerts to the right person in the right channel.

Acceptance Criteria:

System routes messages across three channels: text (SMS), Slack, and email

Routing rules are configured per BlueWave's SOPs: who gets what message type, department-specific rules, and urgency tiers

Routing rules are configurable without code changes

Each message includes contextual information from the relevant ticket

Dependency: BlueWave must document their internal messaging SOPs (see §14)

5.4.2 Daily Tear Sheet Notifications

Priority: Should (aligned with outbound screening activation)

Description: Daily summary notifications to the recruiting team via Slack with updates on candidate activity, tear sheet additions, and screening results.

Acceptance Criteria:

Daily digest notification sent to a configurable Slack channel at a scheduled time

Digest includes: new candidates screened, tear sheet additions, notable interactions, and any flagged issues

Notification links directly to relevant Bullhorn records

5.5 Audit and Reporting Interface (MVP)

5.5.1 Agent Action Audit Trail

Priority: Must

Description: Every agent action is logged and traceable. The BlueWave team can review agent behavior, flag errors, and provide feedback that improves the system.

Acceptance Criteria:

Full action trace for every agent interaction (decision tree path, data accessed, outputs generated)

Audit interface is CMS-level simple — usable by BlueWave's non-technical team

Weekly review workflow is supported (review last week's interactions, flag errors, approve corrections)

5.5.2 Error Flagging and Feedback Loop

Priority: Must

Description: BlueWave team members can flag incorrect agent actions and provide corrections that are incorporated into the agent's behavior.

Acceptance Criteria:

Any team member can flag an agent action as incorrect from the audit interface

Flagged actions include a correction note explaining what should have happened

Corrections are reviewed and incorporated into agent context/rules

System tracks correction history to identify recurring error patterns

Data Quality Considerations

Bullhorn data quality varies. LinkedIn profiles may be outdated, resumes stale. The system must handle incomplete or inconsistent candidate records gracefully — not fail silently on missing fields.

Cross-referencing candidates across source systems is the industry's biggest gap and BlueWave's core opportunity. The system should be designed to handle candidate deduplication across Bullhorn records originating from different sources.

Call audio quality will vary: mobile calls, speakerphone, background noise. The voice pipeline must be tested against realistic audio conditions, not clean studio recordings.

Data Privacy and Ownership

Closed-loop deployment required. No candidate data, call recordings, or business data should be exposed to external systems beyond explicitly integrated partners.

BlueWave owns all data processed through Swell. Mirror Factory has no data retention rights beyond what is needed for active development and support.

Cloud vs. local trade-off: Cloud models (including AWS Bedrock) are not fully air-gapped. This trade-off must be explicitly evaluated and accepted during Design & Development. If unacceptable, local models (Gemma 26B+) are viable with a strong validation pipeline.

8. User Experience Surfaces

8.1 External Voice — Caller-Facing

The external voice experience is the primary interface for all inbound callers. It must feel natural, professional, and human-like. This is the highest-risk UX surface because voice quality directly affects caller trust and willingness to engage.

Experience Goals:

Callers should not feel like they are navigating a phone tree or talking to a robot

Conversations should be open-ended and adaptive, not scripted menus

The agent should handle natural conversational patterns: interruptions, corrections, pauses, and topic changes

Voice quality (latency, naturalness, tone) must support a professional business conversation

Voice Latency Budget:

Humans notice pauses after ~300 milliseconds. Conversations feel broken above 800ms. The voice pipeline has three latency-critical components:

Component

Target Latency

Notes

Speech-to-text (STT)

100–300ms

Real-time streaming preferred over batch

LLM inference

200–500ms

Streaming token output reduces perceived latency

Text-to-speech (TTS)

100–300ms

First-audio latency more important than total generation time

Total pipeline

<800ms

Target for natural conversation feel



Turn-taking behavior (detecting when the caller has finished speaking vs. pausing mid-thought) is a critical design decision. Semantic turn detection reduces response times compared to silence-based detection but requires more sophisticated implementation. This will be evaluated during Design & Development.

Interaction Pattern:

Call connects → Agent greets caller with a branded introduction

Agent asks open-ended questions to determine intent

Agent classifies intent and routes accordingly

If routing to a human: agent provides context summary and transfers the call

If resolving directly: agent handles the request and confirms resolution

If transitioning to Recruiter Agent: seamless handoff within the same call

8.2 Internal Messaging — Team-Facing

The internal messaging experience is how BlueWave's team receives agent notifications, ticket summaries, and escalation alerts. It is not a chat interface — it is a routing layer that delivers the right information to the right person in the right channel.

Experience Goals:

Team members receive information where they already work (text, Slack, or email) — no new app to check

Message format is contextual to the recipient and the situation

Urgency tiers are respected: urgent escalations via text, routine updates via Slack, formal communications via email

Information density is appropriate — enough context to act, not a wall of text

Interaction Pattern:

Agent generates a notification (new ticket, escalation, daily digest, etc.)

Messaging bus determines recipient and channel based on SOP rules

Message is formatted with contextual summary appropriate to the recipient's role

Recipient can act directly from the notification (e.g., click through to Bullhorn record)



9. Phased Roadmap

Target Start: Mid-April 2026

Objectives:

Progressive delegation: start with low-stakes call types, expand as confidence builds

Continued development and refinement based on real-world usage

Continued parallel development of outbound screening

Continuous QA — bugs and edge cases captured and resolved in real time

Milestone Gate — Exit Criteria for Phase 2:

☐ System has handled a minimum of [TBD] live inbound calls

Milestone Gate — Exit Criteria for Phase 3:

☐ Cost controls verified for per-click sourcing tools

☐ Productization assessment complete (internal tool vs. licensable product decision)

☐ Additional tools scoped and prioritized for next engagement cycle



10. Testing & QA Strategy

The Challenge

Voice-based AI systems present unique QA challenges. Unlike text-based agents where millions of automated test instances can be run, voice requires either human interaction or high-quality speech simulation. The external voice experience is the highest-risk surface — caller trust depends entirely on voice quality and conversational naturalness.

QA Approach Options

Three approaches were discussed. The recommended approach is a hybrid of options 2 and 3, with the exact mix determined by BlueWave's QA budget.

Option 1: Internal Testing Only — Mirror Factory and BlueWave team call the system, attempt to break it. Fast and low-cost. Lowest coverage.

Option 2: Hired Human Callers — External callers run scripted and adversarial scenarios. Highest fidelity. Higher cost.

Option 3: ElevenLabs TTS Scripting — Write call scripts, use text-to-speech to simulate callers at scale. Middle-ground: higher coverage than internal, lower fidelity than real humans.

Recommended Approach

A hybrid of Options 2 and 3. The exact ratio depends on budget:

Higher budget: Robust human caller program + TTS automation for regression. Pre-launch target: ~100+ scripted test calls.

Lower budget: TTS-heavy with targeted human testing for critical paths. Pre-launch target: ~50+ scripted test calls.

Both are viable. The plans look very different. Budget is a design input for the QA plan.

Pre-Launch Testing Requirements

Before any live deployment (Phase 1 → Phase 2 transition):

All three routing paths (Sales, Admin, Candidates) tested end-to-end

Recruiter Agent screening validated against at least 3 job types

Edge cases tested: unclear intent, caller frustration, immediate human request, repeat callers, wrong number

Ticketing validated: creation, progressive fill, merging, contextual summaries

Messaging bus validated: correct channel, correct person

Voice quality validated across environments (mobile, landline, speakerphone, background noise)

Success Metrics

Success metrics will be defined during Design & Development (see §14). Candidate metrics:

Call resolution rate (% resolved without human escalation)

Time-to-qualified-shortlist (outbound screening)

Recruiter hours saved per week

Candidate satisfaction signal (qualitative, from recruiter feedback)

Ticket accuracy (% rated "useful" by receiving team member)

Error rate (% of calls requiring post-hoc correction)





11. Out of Scope

Website chatbot. Discussed during discovery but designated as an Expansion item, not MVP.

Direct sourcing tool integration. LinkedIn Recruiter, ZipRecruiter, Indeed, and Dice excluded until cost controls are established.

Sales tools and compliance tools. Future possibilities, not part of this engagement.

Multi-tenant architecture. Out of scope until the productization decision is made (Phase 3 gate).

ADP integration. Agent provides self-service instructions from knowledge base only.

Go Marketing / Adam coordination. Not in scope unless Charlie explicitly directs.

Training and change management. Mirror Factory supports onboarding; formal training programs are not scoped deliverables.



12. Constraints & Dependencies

Data Privacy

BlueWave requires a private, closed-loop AI deployment. No candidate data, call recordings, or business data should be exposed externally. Cloud models are not fully air-gapped — this trade-off must be evaluated during Design & Development.

No Internal Engineering Staff

BlueWave has no engineering or IT team. Charlie Lewis and Connor Meehan are the technical POCs. All tooling must be CMS-level simple. Mirror Factory provides ongoing service, not just a code delivery.

Per-Click Sourcing Cost Controls

AI agents must not have uncontrolled access to per-click platforms. Budget guardrails, exclusion rules, and cost caps are hard prerequisites for the outbound screening component.

Maintenance Model

BlueWave expects CMS-like self-service for routine operations with a service agreement for complex changes. This is a hybrid consulting and platform engagement with ongoing support.

Voice QA Limitations

Voice QA cannot be fully automated. The QA approach and budget directly affect pilot quality and timeline.



Client Technical Capacity

Charlie and Connor's availability for additional collaborative design and brainstorming sessions may be a direct dependency on the Phase 1 timeline.

Existing Systems

Bullhorn API access level needs confirmation. Internal messaging SOPs are not formalized. Call routing logic has not been documented at the department level. These are design inputs for Phase 1.

15. Monitoring & Operations

Post-Launch Monitoring Plan

AI systems degrade over time as the world changes beneath them. Unlike traditional software that works until something breaks, AI models and agent behaviors drift. This section defines the monitoring infrastructure that must be planned during Design & Development and operational during the Pilot.

Agent Performance Monitoring:

Track routing accuracy (did calls reach the right department?)

Track screening quality (do recruiter-screened candidates match agent-screened assessments?)

Track escalation rate (is the agent escalating too much or too little?)

Track voice quality metrics (latency, dropped calls, caller hang-up rate during agent conversation)

Model/Prompt Performance:

Monitor LLM response quality over time — detect drift in conversation quality or routing accuracy

Version all prompts and context profiles; compare performance across versions

Establish baseline metrics during Pilot; alert on deviations beyond acceptable thresholds

Operational Dashboard:

Daily summary: calls handled, calls escalated, tickets created, errors flagged

Weekly review: agent action audit, correction patterns, performance trends

Accessible to BlueWave's non-technical team (CMS-level simplicity)

Retraining and Improvement Triggers

90-Day Post-Pilot Review

BlueWave Resource Partners x Mirror Factory

Swell — Product Engagement Proposal



Prepared by: Mirror Factory

Prepared for: BlueWave Resource Partners

Date: April 7, 2026

Revision: 0

1. Swell (The BlueWave AI Platform)

Mirror Factory proposes a phased product development engagement to design, build, and pilot Swell: a proprietary AI-powered call triage and candidate intelligence system for BlueWave Resource Partners. 

Swell answers a simple question: what if every call to BlueWave’s main number was answered instantly, routed intelligently, and produced a contextual record that made the next human interaction better?

The system has two core components. 

Inbound call triage: an AI receptionist answers calls, determines intent through natural conversation, and routes callers to the right department. When a candidate calls, the system transitions to a recruiter agent that leads a pre-screening conversation, evaluates the candidate, and writes structured data directly to Bullhorn. Every call generates a progressive ticket that fills with context as the conversation unfolds.

Outbound candidate screening: when BlueWave receives a new job requisition, Swell screens hundreds of candidates simultaneously against the real qualifying criteria and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour.

Swell integrates directly with BlueWave’s existing systems: Bullhorn, RingCentral, and Slack. No data leaves your ecosystem. BlueWave owns all custom-built Swell IP outright, including full source code and documentation, with no vendor lock-in.





2. Goals and Success Criteria

Eliminate low-value inbound call handling. Calls are triaged by an AI receptionist and routed to the right person or resolved without human intervention.

Pre-screen candidates before human follow-up. A recruiter agent captures structured evaluation data and writes it to Bullhorn, so recruiters start with context.

Create a native ticketing system. Every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries tailored to the recipient.

Establish BlueWave-owned software IP. Swell is a separable asset owned entirely by BlueWave.

Validate through a controlled pilot. A 90-day internal pilot with progressive delegation, expanding as confidence builds.

3. Engagement Plan

Phase 1: Design, Discovery, and Prototyping (4 to 6 weeks)

Mirror Factory embeds into BlueWave’s operational environment. We observe real workflows, work with real Bullhorn data, and iterate rapidly through design and prototyping cycles: direct observation, iterative design sessions with key stakeholders, working prototypes against real call scenarios, and hands-on validation.

Immersive discovery. Map call routing logic across Sales, Admin/HR, and Candidate paths. Document messaging SOPs. Confirm Bullhorn and RingCentral API capabilities.

Agent architecture and prototyping. Build the AI receptionist and recruiter agent. Establish context profiles, guardrails, and escalation rules.

Integration development. Connect to Bullhorn (read/write candidate records, tear sheets) and RingCentral (inbound call routing). Set up Slack notifications.

Ticketing system. Progressive ticket creation, repeat caller merging, and contextual handoff summaries.

Pilot preparation. Define the testing strategy, success metrics, and Phase 2 milestones.

Phase 2: Development, Pilot, and Consulting (~90 days)

Continued development of both inbound and outbound systems alongside a live internal pilot. Weekly consulting with your team throughout.

Internal pilot. Launch inbound triage for live calls with progressive delegation, starting with low-stakes call types.

Continued development and QA. Refine agent behavior based on real-world performance. Execute QA with human callers and scripted simulations.

Outbound screening development. Requisition ingestion, candidate matching, and shortlist generation designed in parallel with inbound pilot.

Consulting and support. Weekly reviews, agent performance reporting, error flagging, and team training on the audit interface.

Phase 3: Expansion and Productization (future, not priced)

Full activation of outbound screening, where the system contacts hundreds of candidates simultaneously and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour. Evaluation of packaging Swell 1.0 for licensing to other staffing firms. Design of additional AI tools for sales, compliance, and business development. Implementation of system learning so agents improve from placement outcomes and recruiter feedback. Phase 3 is where the initial investment compounds: the system built for BlueWave becomes a product that generates value independently.

2. Project Overview

Project Title

Project Aqueduct — AI-Powered Call Triage & Candidate Intelligence System

Brief Description

A proprietary AI software system for BlueWave Resource Partners that handles inbound call triage and routing across multiple caller types (clients, employees, contractors, candidates), integrates with Bullhorn (ATS/CRM) and RingCentral (phone), and writes all context back to existing systems. The system is architected in two layers: Layers (Mirror Factory’s reusable platform IP providing context management, agent profiles, rules, integrations, and authoring tools) and Aqueduct (Charlie’s IP — the orchestration layer handling routing logic, native ticketing, decision-making, and staffing-specific integrations). The system has two core components: inbound triage and outbound candidate screening. The MVP will focus on inbound triage as proof of concept; outbound screening will be developed in parallel and activated once the inbound foundation is validated. Charlie’s strategic motivation is to own software IP that increases company valuation ahead of a planned exit in ~2 years.

Project Goals / Outcomes

1. Eliminate low-value inbound call handling — route calls intelligently so recruiters, admins, and sales staff only handle calls that require human judgment.
2. Increase recruiter capacity 5–10x — from ~1,000 candidates at capacity to intelligently managing 5,000–10,000 through AI-assisted screening and triage.
3. Compress time-to-qualified-shortlist — from days of manual sorting to under one hour after receiving a new job req (enabled by outbound screening component).
4. Create standalone software IP — architected as a separable asset on the balance sheet to maximize exit valuation multiple.
5. Build a native ticketing and context system — every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries tailored to the recipient, enabling warm handoffs to humans.

Project Requirements / Needs

Inbound call routing across three paths: Sales, Admin/HR, and Candidates (new and existing)

AI Receptionist agent that introduces, gathers intent, and routes calls based on configurable rules

Recruiter agent that switches into pre-screening mode for candidate calls — leads conversation, evaluates against rubric, captures structured output

Native ticketing system: every call creates a blank ticket that fills progressively; repeat callers merge automatically; contextual summaries tailored per recipient

External voice UX: high-quality, natural conversational experience for callers

Internal messaging bus: central routing layer across text, Slack, and email with SOP-driven rules for who gets what message type

Bullhorn integration: read/write on candidate records, tear sheets, job requisitions, call transcripts, and evaluation data

RingCentral integration: call routing, telephony infrastructure

Context management layer (Layers): agent profiles, rules, integrations, authoring tools — reusable across engagements

Audit and reporting interface: BlueWave team can review agent actions, flag errors, and improve over time

Progressive delegation model: start with low-stakes call types, expand as confidence builds during pilot

Data ownership: closed-loop deployment, no external data exposure, BlueWave owns all Aqueduct IP

3. Technical Requirements

Platform

Cloud-hosted or local deployment (TBD based on security and cost analysis). Options discussed: AWS Bedrock (cloud), local models (Gemma 26B+), or hybrid. Deployment decision affects security posture and operating costs.

Tech Stack

Implementation likely Python (similar to existing Kyle setup) or Next.js modeled on open-source agent frameworks (OpenAI/Anthropic patterns). Voice model TBD: cloud (ElevenLabs, Bedrock) vs. local. Standards for building orchestrators to be established during this engagement.

Integrations

Required:
• Bullhorn (ATS/CRM) — core integration, read/write on candidate records, tear sheets, job reqs. ~200K candidate records in database. API access level needs confirmation.
• RingCentral — telephony and call routing infrastructure. RingCentral AIR evaluated but lacks Bullhorn integration and data portability.
• Slack — internal notifications, daily tear sheet updates, agent alerts.
• BlueWave website job board — hosted by third party, syncs to Bullhorn, distributes to ~100 job boards via Haley/Daily Marketing.

Deferred (outbound screening component):
• LinkedIn Recruiter, ZipRecruiter, Indeed, Dice — sourcing tools with per-click cost structures; will not be opened to agents initially due to cost controls needed.

Data Requirements

Closed-loop / private AI deployment. No candidate or client data exposed externally. System will access internal tools and candidate data; security parameters to be defined upfront. Bullhorn data export confirmed possible before contract end. Cross-referencing candidates across source systems is the biggest gap in the industry — this is where the opportunity lives.

Security Requirements

Private/closed-loop deployment required. Cloud models (including AWS Bedrock) still touch external infrastructure — not fully air-gapped. Local models (26B+) viable if testing/validation pipeline is strong. Security implications of cloud vs. local to be resolved during design phase.

Scalability Requirements

Current state: ~8–10 inbound calls/day, ~18–20 recruiters, ~200K candidates in Bullhorn. System must scale to support outbound screening (hundreds to thousands of candidate contacts per job req simultaneously).

Performance Requirements

Voice latency must support natural conversation flow. Outbound screening target: qualified candidate shortlist within 1 hour of new req receipt.

Description of User Experience

Two distinct UX surfaces:

1. External Voice (caller-facing): Inbound callers interact with an AI receptionist that introduces itself, determines intent through natural conversation, and routes to the appropriate path (Sales, Admin, or Candidates). Dedicated QA testing plan required (see Testing section).

2. Internal Messaging (team-facing): A central messaging bus routes notifications, summaries, and escalations to BlueWave staff across text, Slack, and email. Routing follows SOPs: who gets texted vs. emailed, department-specific rules, urgency tiers. Ticket summaries are contextual to the recipient (Charlie gets escalation context; recruiting team gets candidate-fit summaries).

Design Inspiration / Competitors

RingCentral AI Receptionist (AIR) is the closest off-the-shelf analog — but lacks Bullhorn integration and data portability. Tenzo AI for candidate screening with Bullhorn integration. No existing product handles multi-caller-type inbound triage with deep staffing CRM integration.

BlueWave’s non-technical team needs a CMS-level-simplicity interface to review agent decisions weekly, flag incorrect responses, and improve agent behavior over time. Agents modeled like interns: given instructions, deployed, and improved through feedback loops. Full trace of every agent action back to a decision.

Technical POCs: Charlie and Connor. Adam (Go Marketing, external) handles website/marketing but is not in scope unless Charlie explicitly directs coordination.

Current State

Inbound calls handled manually. Recruiters spend 75–80% of time on unqualified candidates. Manual sorting through Bullhorn database for job req matching (SpaceX example: 500 applicants, days to produce a shortlist of 30). Team at capacity — can’t grow volume without adding headcount or augmenting digitally. SaaS tools evaluated (Bullhorn Amplify, RingCentral AIR, LinkedIn Recruiter, Tenzo, Whippy, Brilo, Paradox) — none solve the full multi-caller-type triage + Bullhorn integration problem.

Desired Future State

Every call produces a contextual ticket for warm human handoff. Team capacity multiplied 3–5x without proportional headcount growth. Standalone software IP on balance sheet for exit valuation.

Known Constraints

No internal engineering staff. First custom software build. Team uses text/Slack/email interchangeably with no formalized SOPs. Per-click sourcing tools (Indeed, Dice) need cost controls before agent access. Charlie less involved in day-to-day — real users are Laura, Connor, Rachel, Kourtney, recruiters.

Existing Processes / Tools

Bullhorn (ATS/CRM, ~200K candidates), RingCentral (phones), Slack (internal comms), ADP (payroll/benefits), BlueWave website job board (third-party hosted, syncs to Bullhorn, distributes to ~100 boards), LinkedIn Recruiter, ZipRecruiter, Indeed, Dice (sourcing).

6. Project Constraints

Budget

TBD — Charlie stated he does not yet know what this costs. No explicit budget range given. Internal Mirror Factory estimate: $75K–$200K+ depending on scope (internal tool vs. productized software). Value-based pricing model under development. Budget range needed to inform QA plan design and pilot scope.

Timeline

Phased quarterly cadence:
The project follows named phases:

Design & Development (estimated 4–6 weeks) — establish the design and development plan, build the MVP focused on inbound triage.
Pilot (estimated ~90 days) — internal pilot focused on inbound triage, continued development of outbound screening, decision gate at end.
Expansion — activate outbound screening, evaluate productization, design additional tools.

Exact phase durations and project milestones will be defined during the Design & Development phase. Exit timeline (~2 years from now) is the strategic constraint — software IP must be built, proven, and demonstrable before exit window.

Other Constraints

• Data privacy: closed-loop deployment, no external data exposure
• No internal engineering staff — system must be maintainable by non-technical team via CMS-like interface
• Voice QA cannot be fully automated — requires human or simulated caller testing
• Per-click sourcing tools need cost controls before outbound screening agent access
• IP ownership: BlueWave owns all Aqueduct IP; Mirror Factory retains Layers IP

9. Questions for Client

Discovery Gaps

What is BlueWave’s approximate annual cost for recruiter time spent on unqualified candidates? Even a rough estimate helps us frame the value of the system and inform pricing. (Targets: Cost of Inaction signal)

Is there a number you’d attach to the value of compressing time-to-qualified-shortlist from days to under an hour? What would that be worth per job req, or annually? (Targets: Value of Outcome signal)

Scope & Technical

Is Project Aqueduct purely an internal tool, or do you envision licensing or selling it to other staffing firms? This significantly affects architecture decisions (multi-tenancy, portability, documentation standards).

Can you confirm the Bullhorn API access level BlueWave currently has? Specifically: read/write on candidate records, tear sheets, and job requisitions.

What are the 3–5 qualifying criteria most commonly used across job types when your recruiters screen candidates? This defines the initial screening agent logic.

Walk us through the full call routing tree: when someone calls the main number today, who answers, what questions do they ask, and how do they decide where to route? We need this documented for each department path (Sales, Admin/HR, Recruiting).

What internal messaging SOPs exist? When does someone get a text vs. a Slack message vs. an email? Are there department-specific rules or urgency tiers?

Budget & Engagement

What is your budget range for this engagement? Frame: budget determines whether we invest in robust human QA testing, automated TTS testing, or a hybrid. Both paths are viable but look very different. This is a design input, not a qualification question.

Who beyond Charlie needs to approve budget and scope? Should we plan to present to Michael Bowles (CFO) or the full leadership team?

What does Charlie and Connor’s availability look like for whiteboard sessions, Bullhorn walkthroughs, and ongoing design feedback during the build phase?

10. Internal Notes

Internal use only — not for client distribution.

Deal Signal

Full product build (phased), hybrid consulting + platform. Large: $100K+. Confidence: Medium (no budget discussed; inferred from revenue, exit motivation, comparable SaaS spend).

Qualification Status

Qualified — proceed to ROM

Recommended Next Step

Generate ROM estimate. Structure as phased engagement with named phases: Design & Development, Pilot, Expansion. Project and product milestones to be defined during the Design & Development phase. Include value-based pricing framework alongside cost breakdown. Align internally on pricing model and communication approach before sending.

Pipeline Notes

Relationship dynamics: Strong existing trust. Watch for friend-discount trap — relationship should earn trust and access, not pressure to undervalue work. Charlie is motivated by financial outcomes and will respect a well-justified price.

Strategic value: First client engagement for Layers platform. This engagement establishes the pattern for future staffing vertical clients and validates Mirror Factory’s consulting + platform model.

Cross-sell potential: Website chatbot, shared inbox, sales tools, compliance workflows — expanding engagement as Aqueduct matures.

Concerns: Pricing communication has caused recent deals to stall or fall short — team flagged this as a gap that needs to be fixed before this proposal goes out. Value is there; problem is how it’s being communicated. Also: BlueWave has no prior custom software experience — expectations on iterative development process may need calibration.

  1. What is BlueWave’s approximate annual cost for recruiter time spent on unqualified candidates? Even a rough estimate helps us frame the value of the system and inform pricing. (Targets: Cost of Inaction signal)
  2. Is there a number you’d attach to the value of compressing time-to-qualified-shortlist from days to under an hour? What would that be worth per job req, or annually? (Targets: Value of Outcome signal)

Scope & Technical

  1. Is Project Aqueduct purely an internal tool, or do you envision licensing or selling it to other staffing firms? This significantly affects architecture decisions (multi-tenancy, portability, documentation standards).
  2. Can you confirm the Bullhorn API access level BlueWave currently has? Specifically: read/write on candidate records, tear sheets, and job requisitions.
  3. What are the 3–5 qualifying criteria most commonly used across job types when your recruiters screen candidates? This defines the initial screening agent logic.
  4. Walk us through the full call routing tree: when someone calls the main number today, who answers, what questions do they ask, and how do they decide where to route? We need this documented for each department path (Sales, Admin/HR, Recruiting).
  5. What internal messaging SOPs exist? When does someone get a text vs. a Slack message vs. an email? Are there department-specific rules or urgency tiers?

Budget & Engagement

  1. What is your budget range for this engagement? Frame: budget determines whether we invest in robust human QA testing, automated TTS testing, or a hybrid. Both paths are viable but look very different. This is a design input, not a qualification question.
  2. Who beyond Charlie needs to approve budget and scope? Should we plan to present to Michael Bowles (CFO) or the full leadership team?
  3. What does Charlie and Connor’s availability look like for whiteboard sessions, Bullhorn walkthroughs, and ongoing design feedback during the build phase?

10. Internal Notes

Internal use only — not for client distribution.

Deal Signal

Full product build (phased), hybrid consulting + platform. Large: $100K+. Confidence: Medium (no budget discussed; inferred from revenue, exit motivation, comparable SaaS spend).

Qualification Status

Qualified — proceed to ROM

Recommended Next Step

Generate ROM estimate. Structure as phased engagement with named phases: Design & Development, Pilot, Expansion. Project and product milestones to be defined during the Design & Development phase. Include value-based pricing framework alongside cost breakdown. Align internally on pricing model and communication approach before sending.

Pipeline Notes

Relationship dynamics: Strong existing trust. Watch for friend-discount trap — relationship should earn trust and access, not pressure to undervalue work. Charlie is motivated by financial outcomes and will respect a well-justified price. Strategic value: First client engagement for Layers platform. This engagement establishes the pattern for future staffing vertical clients and validates Mirror Factory’s consulting + platform model. Cross-sell potential: Website chatbot, shared inbox, sales tools, compliance workflows — expanding engagement as Aqueduct matures. Concerns: Pricing communication has caused recent deals to stall or fall short — team flagged this as a gap that needs to be fixed before this proposal goes out. Value is there; problem is how it’s being communicated. Also: BlueWave has no prior custom software experience — expectations on iterative development process may need calibration.

Meeting Source

Granola meeting notes: “BlueWave AI Discussion” (Mar 31, 2026 — discovery with client) and “Discussing BlueWave Project” (Apr 3, 2026 — internal Mirror Factory debrief). Full transcripts available.

BlueWave Resource Partners x Mirror Factory

Swell — Product Engagement Proposal



Prepared by: Mirror Factory

Prepared for: BlueWave Resource Partners

Date: April 7, 2026

Revision: 0

1. Swell (The BlueWave AI Platform)

Mirror Factory proposes a phased product development engagement to design, build, and pilot Swell: a proprietary AI-powered call triage and candidate intelligence system for BlueWave Resource Partners. 

Swell answers a simple question: what if every call to BlueWave’s main number was answered instantly, routed intelligently, and produced a contextual record that made the next human interaction better?

The system has two core components. 

Inbound call triage: an AI receptionist answers calls, determines intent through natural conversation, and routes callers to the right department. When a candidate calls, the system transitions to a recruiter agent that leads a pre-screening conversation, evaluates the candidate, and writes structured data directly to Bullhorn. Every call generates a progressive ticket that fills with context as the conversation unfolds.

Outbound candidate screening: when BlueWave receives a new job requisition, Swell screens hundreds of candidates simultaneously against the real qualifying criteria and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour.

Swell integrates directly with BlueWave’s existing systems: Bullhorn, RingCentral, and Slack. No data leaves your ecosystem. BlueWave owns all custom-built Swell IP outright, including full source code and documentation, with no vendor lock-in.





2. Goals and Success Criteria

Eliminate low-value inbound call handling. Calls are triaged by an AI receptionist and routed to the right person or resolved without human intervention.

Pre-screen candidates before human follow-up. A recruiter agent captures structured evaluation data and writes it to Bullhorn, so recruiters start with context.

Create a native ticketing system. Every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries tailored to the recipient.

Establish BlueWave-owned software IP. Swell is a separable asset owned entirely by BlueWave.

Validate through a controlled pilot. A 90-day internal pilot with progressive delegation, expanding as confidence builds.

3. Engagement Plan

Phase 1: Design, Discovery, and Prototyping (4 to 6 weeks)

Mirror Factory embeds into BlueWave’s operational environment. We observe real workflows, work with real Bullhorn data, and iterate rapidly through design and prototyping cycles: direct observation, iterative design sessions with key stakeholders, working prototypes against real call scenarios, and hands-on validation.

Immersive discovery. Map call routing logic across Sales, Admin/HR, and Candidate paths. Document messaging SOPs. Confirm Bullhorn and RingCentral API capabilities.

Agent architecture and prototyping. Build the AI receptionist and recruiter agent. Establish context profiles, guardrails, and escalation rules.

Integration development. Connect to Bullhorn (read/write candidate records, tear sheets) and RingCentral (inbound call routing). Set up Slack notifications.

Ticketing system. Progressive ticket creation, repeat caller merging, and contextual handoff summaries.

Pilot preparation. Define the testing strategy, success metrics, and Phase 2 milestones.

Phase 2: Development, Pilot, and Consulting (~90 days)

Continued development of both inbound and outbound systems alongside a live internal pilot. Weekly consulting with your team throughout.

Internal pilot. Launch inbound triage for live calls with progressive delegation, starting with low-stakes call types.

Continued development and QA. Refine agent behavior based on real-world performance. Execute QA with human callers and scripted simulations.

Outbound screening development. Requisition ingestion, candidate matching, and shortlist generation designed in parallel with inbound pilot.

Consulting and support. Weekly reviews, agent performance reporting, error flagging, and team training on the audit interface.

Phase 3: Expansion and Productization (future, not priced)

Full activation of outbound screening, where the system contacts hundreds of candidates simultaneously and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour. Evaluation of packaging Swell 1.0 for licensing to other staffing firms. Design of additional AI tools for sales, compliance, and business development. Implementation of system learning so agents improve from placement outcomes and recruiter feedback. Phase 3 is where the initial investment compounds: the system built for BlueWave becomes a product that generates value independently.
