title: Competitive Landscape
extractedText:
Competitive landscape

Why existing tools don’t solve BlueWave’s problem — and why building Swell is the right move



The gap in the market

BlueWave needs a system that does three things simultaneously: triages inbound calls across multiple caller types (clients, employees, contractors, candidates), conducts structured candidate pre-screening via voice, and writes all context back into Bullhorn as the system of record. No existing product does all three.

This brief maps each competitor against BlueWave’s actual requirements. The conclusion isn’t that these tools are bad. It’s that they weren’t built for what BlueWave needs — and none of them create ownable IP.



RingCentral AI Receptionist (AIR)

What it does: RingCentral AIR is an AI phone agent that answers calls, handles FAQs, routes callers, schedules appointments, captures leads, and sends follow-up texts. Launched February 2025, over 3,000 businesses now use it. In March 2026, RingCentral launched AIR Pro — an omnichannel agentic version with a no-code agent builder and 100+ enterprise integrations, starting with healthcare. Pricing runs $59–$69/month with 100 included minutes and $0.50/minute overage.

What it does well: AIR is the closest off-the-shelf product to Swell’s inbound triage capability. It handles natural conversation (not menu trees), routes calls by name and context, and works with any SIP-based phone system. One healthcare provider reported $204,000 in monthly revenue increase.

Where it falls short for BlueWave

No Bullhorn integration. AIR connects to Salesforce and HubSpot but has no native Bullhorn connector. The existing RingCentral-Bullhorn connector (via Tenfold) provides click-to-call only — AIR’s AI layer cannot read candidate records or write screening results.

No candidate pre-screening. AIR routes calls and answers questions — it doesn’t lead structured screening conversations or evaluate candidates against a rubric.

No multi-caller-type awareness. AIR treats all callers the same. BlueWave’s main line handles four distinct caller types that each need different handling logic.

No outbound capability. AIR is inbound-only with no outbound calling or candidate engagement features.

No IP ownership. Subscribing to AIR means the capability leaves when the subscription ends.

Bottom line: AIR is a strong general-purpose receptionist, but it’s a horizontal product with no staffing DNA, no Bullhorn integration, and no path to IP ownership.



Bullhorn Amplify

What it does: Amplify is Bullhorn’s own AI suite, launched May 2025. It automates sourcing, matching, and outreach inside the ATS. Customers report a 51% increase in job submissions and a 22% increase in fill rates. It includes an AI Voice Screener, but this is asynchronous — candidates complete screening on their own schedule via a link, not during a live call. Pricing is not publicly disclosed; estimated total for a mid-size agency: $20K–$60K+/year.

What it does well: Amplify lives inside Bullhorn with the deepest possible integration. For text-based recruiting tasks — summarizing candidates, drafting outreach, matching profiles — it’s genuinely useful. It leverages 22 billion data points across Bullhorn’s platform.

Where it falls short for BlueWave

No live voice or inbound call handling. Amplify has no phone capability. It doesn’t answer calls, route callers, or conduct live voice conversations.

Screening is asynchronous only. Candidates receive a link and complete screening on their own time. Fundamentally different from Swell’s live AI phone screens.

No progressive ticketing. Doesn’t create tickets that build context across multiple interactions.

Platform dependency. Capabilities are controlled by Bullhorn’s roadmap. No IP ownership.

Bottom line: Amplify is complementary to Swell, not competitive with it. Useful for in-platform productivity but doesn’t touch live voice, inbound triage, or IP creation.



Whippy AI

What it does: Whippy is the most feature-comparable product to Swell discovered in this research. It offers an AI Recruiter Agent (screening, scoring, scheduling on apply), an AI Receptionist Agent (answers FAQs, routes calls, captures leads), and Voice AI (natural phone conversations for pre-screening in 50+ languages). The platform is omnichannel — SMS, email, WhatsApp, VoIP, and web chat. Whippy has a deep native Bullhorn integration that syncs scores, notes, transcripts, and call logs automatically. Pricing is contract-based; reference points suggest ~$50K/year for initial contracts, scaling to six figures.

What it does well: Whippy comes closest to covering Swell’s full feature set. CCS Staffing reported $1.64M in annual savings (equivalent of 25 recruiters), and Link Staffing achieved 95% reduction in interview costs with 20x faster engagement. The company is bootstrapped and profitable.

Where it falls short for BlueWave

SaaS platform — no IP ownership. BlueWave would own no source code, no custom logic, and no proprietary asset. For a firm planning an exit in two years, this is the critical difference.

Generic across industries. Whippy serves staffing, pharmacy, home services, and others. The AI isn’t custom-trained on BlueWave’s specific workflows, call routing SOPs, or Bullhorn data.

No progressive ticketing. Handles calls and syncs data, but doesn’t create compound interaction histories with role-specific summaries routed to the right internal person.

Vendor dependency. Features, pricing, and roadmap are controlled by Whippy. If they pivot away from staffing or get acquired, BlueWave has no fallback.

Bottom line: Whippy is the strongest SaaS alternative. If IP ownership weren’t a factor, it would be a serious option. But it creates a recurring expense, not a separable asset.



ConverzAI

What it does: ConverzAI deploys AI “Virtual Recruiters” that conduct live phone conversations at scale — sourcing, screening, qualification, and scheduling. The company raised a $16M Series A in February 2025 (Menlo Ventures, Left Lane Capital) totaling ~$22M. Founded in 2019, ConverzAI has logged 250,000+ candidate conversations with 80%+ satisfaction scores and counts Apex Systems and Malone Workforce Solutions as customers. It integrates with ATS platforms including Bullhorn.

What it does well: ConverzAI has the most mature voice AI for staffing. Clients report time-to-fill reduction from 24 hours to 4 hours and 80% year-over-year growth without adding headcount.

Where it falls short for BlueWave

Candidate screening focus, not multi-caller-type triage. ConverzAI’s inbound capability focuses on candidate interactions — not routing prospective clients to sales, handling admin calls, or resolving ADP password resets.

No progressive ticketing or internal messaging bus. No contextual summaries routed to the right team member in the right channel.

Enterprise SaaS — no IP ownership. Pricing is not public but likely enterprise-tier ($50K–$200K+/year). BlueWave would own nothing.

Bottom line: ConverzAI is the best-funded voice AI startup focused on staffing, but it’s a screening tool — not a unified communications layer. No IP ownership.



Tenzo AI

What it does: Tenzo conducts structured voice and video interviews, scores candidates against configurable rubrics, and writes results back into Bullhorn. It raised a $4M seed round and has deep Bullhorn integration. Pricing is estimated at $20K–$50K+/year.

What it does well: Deep Bullhorn integration, support for both phone and video interviews, 75% screening cost reductions claimed, and 40+ languages with mid-call switching.

Where it falls short for BlueWave

Outbound and screening only. Doesn’t answer the main phone line, triage inbound calls, or route callers to departments.

No internal messaging or ticketing. No progressive tickets or notification routing.

Early stage with dual business model. Tenzo also operates as a staffing agency — a potential conflict of interest with staffing firm customers.

No IP ownership. SaaS subscription with no transferable asset.

Bottom line: Tenzo is competitive with Swell’s outbound screening component but has zero inbound triage capability.



Other competitors and emerging startups

The staffing AI market is expanding rapidly — VC investment in AI recruiting startups exceeded $200M in 2025–2026. Several other tools overlap with pieces of Swell:

Sense HQ is the most established AI talent engagement platform with 1,000+ customers and a native Bullhorn marketplace integration. Voice AI is primarily outbound — it doesn’t function as an inbound receptionist. Estimated: $50K–$150K+/year.

Alex (formerly Apriora, $20M raised, YC-backed) conducts real-time voice and video interviews at scale with Bullhorn sync. Purely an interview tool — no inbound triage.

JobTalk AI has deep Bullhorn integration and handles both inbound applicant screening and outbound re-engagement via voice. One customer logged 723,000 outreach calls. Closest emerging threat after Whippy, but still SaaS.

Paradox (Olivia) was acquired by Workday for $1 billion in October 2025 — a market validation signal. Chat/SMS-first, targets direct employers, and will likely deprioritize non-Workday integrations. Not realistic for a Bullhorn shop.

Purplefish (founded 2024) builds voice AI screening for staffing with native Bullhorn sync. Very early (~8 employees) but experienced founders. One to watch.

Spott (YC W25, $3.2M seed) is building an AI-native ATS/CRM positioned as a Bullhorn replacement. If it gains traction, it could change the integration landscape — though adoption is years away.

None of these tools combine inbound voice triage, outbound screening, progressive ticketing, and internal message routing. Every one is a SaaS subscription.



Why building is the right choice

Every existing tool covers one dimension of what BlueWave needs. The market gap exists at the intersection of five requirements that no single product addresses.



Requirement 1: Multi-caller-type inbound triage. Only Whippy offers something comparable. AIR, Amplify, ConverzAI, Tenzo, and every other tool either lack inbound call handling or can’t classify callers by type.

Requirement 2: Structured candidate pre-screening via voice. Several competitors handle outbound screening (ConverzAI, Tenzo, JobTalk, Sense). None pair it with inbound triage in a single platform.

Requirement 3: Deep Bullhorn read/write integration. Amplify has the deepest (it IS Bullhorn), followed by Whippy, Sense, and Tenzo. But Amplify has no live voice, and the others lack inbound triage.

Requirement 4: Progressive ticketing with contextual handoffs. No competitor creates compound interaction histories with role-specific summaries routed to internal staff.

Requirement 5: Ownable software IP. Every competitor is SaaS. Swell is the only approach where BlueWave owns all source code and custom logic — creating an asset, not an expense.



The industry data reinforces the timing. Bullhorn’s GRID 2026 report found that top-performing staffing firms are 4x more likely to be using AI — up from 2x one year earlier. The advantage is compounding. Yet only 10% of firms have implemented AI across their full workflow. The window for building proprietary AI infrastructure is wide open.

For a $40M firm, off-the-shelf platforms at $200–$600/user/month produce annual SaaS costs of $120K–$360K+ in perpetuity with no IP accumulation. Swell’s investment — $20K (Phase 1) plus $120K–$180K (Phase 2) — reaches cost parity within two years while producing a separable software asset that increases exit valuation.

Swell is designed to fill this gap. It’s not competing with these tools — it’s built for a problem none of them were designed to solve.







This document is part of the Swell engagement proposal prepared by Mirror Factory for BlueWave Resource Partners.

title: Cost of Inaction
extractedText:
The cost of waiting

What BlueWave loses every month without Swell



The numbers below are built from BlueWave’s own data — shared during the March 31 discovery meeting — combined with conservative industry benchmarks. None of these figures require assumptions about Swell’s performance. They describe what the current manual process costs today.



Recruiter labor misallocation

BlueWave has 18–20 recruiters. By the team’s own estimate, recruiters spend 75–80% of their time on unqualified candidates — sorting through databases, reading stale resumes, making calls that go nowhere.

At a conservative fully-loaded cost of $50,000 per recruiter per year (salary, benefits, tools, overhead):



$750,000 / year

20 recruiters × $50,000 × 75% = recruiter time spent on unqualified candidates

That’s $62,500 per month in recruiter labor that produces no placements.

Even at the low end (18 recruiters × $50K × 75%), the number is $675,000/year — $56,250 per month. This isn’t a projection of what Swell will save. It’s a description of what the current process costs right now.



Time-to-shortlist delay

When a major requisition arrives — like the SpaceX example from the discovery meeting (30 electrical engineers, two-week deadline) — it takes days of manual sorting through ~500 applicants before a recruiter even makes the first call.

BlueWave receives 8–10 inbound calls per day on the general line. Many are low-value: ADP password resets, basic status checks, routing calls that landed on the wrong person. These calls pull recruiters and admins away from revenue-generating work.

title: Day in the Life — Swell
extractedText:
A day in the life

How Swell transforms a recruiter’s morning at BlueWave



Today: Monday morning without Swell

This is what a typical Monday looks like for a recruiter at BlueWave right now. The details come from your team’s own description of their daily workflow.



7:45 AM — Connor opens Bullhorn.

A new requisition came in over the weekend: SpaceX needs 30 electrical engineers in Orlando, starting in two weeks. There are roughly 500 candidates in the database who applied to similar roles in the past two months. Connor starts keyword searching.

8:10 AM — The main line rings. ADP password reset.

Connor takes the call, walks the contractor through the reset process, logs it manually. Four minutes gone.

8:22 AM — Another call. Prospective client asking about staffing.

This should go to the sales team, but it came to the general line. Connor takes a message, sends a Slack, goes back to sorting resumes.

8:35 AM — Connor is 30 candidates into the SpaceX sort.

Most don’t meet the basic requirements. Outdated LinkedIn profiles. Stale resumes. No clear signal on availability. He’s reading each one individually.

9:15 AM — A candidate calls about an application from last week.

Connor pulls up the record, checks status, provides an update. The candidate asks about two other open positions. Connor looks those up too. Twelve minutes.

9:30 AM — 80 of 500 candidates reviewed. 6 on the call list.

He hasn’t made a single outbound call yet. The morning is half gone.

10:00 AM — Connor starts calling his shortlist of 6.

Two don’t answer. One is no longer interested. One doesn’t meet a requirement that wasn’t clear from the resume. Two are promising. He takes notes in Bullhorn.

11:30 AM — 2 qualified candidates from 500 records and 3.5 hours of work.

SpaceX needs 30. He’ll repeat this process all week. The other 18 recruiters are doing the same thing for their own requisitions.



Tomorrow: Monday morning with Swell

Same recruiter, same requisition, same start time. The difference is what the system handled before Connor sat down.



7:58 AM — Connor’s phone buzzes. Slack notification from Swell.

“45 screened electrical engineers added to tear sheet for SpaceX Job #2847. 12 confirmed available and interested. Top 5 matched 4/5 qualifying criteria.” The requisition entered Bullhorn at 6:30 AM. Swell screened 500 candidates in 47 minutes.

8:02 AM — Connor opens Bullhorn. The tear sheet is populated.

Each candidate has a structured profile: qualifying criteria scores, availability confirmation, compensation expectations, and a one-paragraph summary. Connor is reading evaluations, not resumes.

8:10 AM — The main line rings. Swell answers.

The AI receptionist identifies an ADP password reset request, walks the contractor through the process, and closes the ticket. Connor’s phone never rings. A ticket appears in the audit log.

8:14 AM — Another call. Prospective client.

Swell identifies the sales intent, captures the caller’s name and company, and routes a contextual ticket to the sales team via text: “New business inquiry from Meridian Corp, asking about engineering staffing in Orlando. Caller: James Torres, VP Operations.” The sales team calls back within 10 minutes.

8:18 AM — A candidate calls about last week’s application.

Swell matches the caller to their Bullhorn record, provides an automated status update, and asks if they’re interested in two other open positions that match their profile. The candidate says yes to one. Swell creates a ticket flagged for recruiter follow-up with updated availability.

8:22 AM — Connor is on his first outbound call.

Twenty-two minutes into his day, he’s talking to a qualified, interested electrical engineer who already confirmed availability and expectations through Swell’s screening. Connor’s job is the relationship: assessing cultural fit, answering questions about SpaceX, and closing.

9:15 AM — Connor has spoken with 4 qualified candidates.

All 4 were pre-screened. He moved 3 forward in the process. He’s working from a tear sheet of 45, not a database of 500.

10:00 AM — Connor reviews the Swell audit log.

8 inbound calls handled. 2 self-service resolutions. 1 sales lead routed. 3 candidate interactions captured. 2 tickets flagged for follow-up. Zero calls required Connor’s direct intervention. He flags one routing decision for review and adds a note.

10:15 AM — More qualified conversations by mid-morning than a full day used to produce.

He still has 40 pre-screened candidates on the tear sheet. He didn’t sort a single resume. He didn’t answer a password reset call. He spent his morning doing what he’s best at: talking to the right people.



The math behind the morning

The scenario above isn’t hypothetical. It’s built from BlueWave’s own description of their current workflow: 500 candidates per major requisition, days of manual sorting, 8 to 10 inbound calls per day on the general line, and 75 to 80 percent of recruiter time spent on unqualified candidates.

Swell compresses the screening cycle from days to under one hour. It eliminates low-value inbound call handling entirely. And it gives every recruiter what Connor got in this scenario: a pre-screened, pre-qualified starting point for every conversation.

Multiply this across 18 to 20 recruiters. Every morning. Every requisition. That’s the compound effect.







This document is part of the Swell engagement proposal prepared by Mirror Factory for BlueWave Resource Partners.

title: FAQ
extractedText:
Frequently asked questions

Common questions about Swell from the BlueWave leadership team



Operations and daily workflow

Will Swell replace our recruiters?

No. Swell is designed to make recruiters more productive, not to replace them. Charlie said it clearly during discovery: the goal is getting humans talking to the right people faster. Swell handles the work that doesn’t require human judgment — sorting through databases, answering password reset calls, routing inquiries to the right department. Your recruiters still do what they’re best at: building relationships, assessing cultural fit, and closing placements. Think of Swell as adding capacity without adding headcount.

What happens to our current phone workflow?

Calls to BlueWave’s main number will be answered by Swell’s AI receptionist instead of a person. The receptionist determines what the caller needs and routes them accordingly — sales inquiries go to the sales team, admin questions get handled or routed to the right person, and candidate calls are either answered directly (status checks) or transitioned to the AI recruiter for pre-screening. If a caller asks to speak to a human at any point, the system transfers them immediately with full context. Nothing is forced through the AI — it’s a first layer, not a wall.

How will the team know what Swell handled?

Every call generates a ticket with a contextual summary. These summaries are delivered to the right person in the right channel — text, Slack, or email — based on your existing communication preferences. Charlie gets escalation context. Recruiters get candidate-fit summaries. Admin gets resolution details. There’s also a weekly audit log where the team reviews what Swell handled, flags anything that looks wrong, and provides corrections that improve the system over time.

Will Swell change how we use Bullhorn?

No. Bullhorn stays your system of record. Swell reads from and writes to Bullhorn — candidate records, tear sheets, job requisitions, call transcripts, and evaluation data. Your recruiters will find new information in Bullhorn (screening results, call summaries, candidate evaluations) but they won’t need to learn a new system or change how they work day to day.

What about our admin processes — ADP questions, benefits calls, status checks?

Swell handles these. When someone calls about an ADP password reset or a benefits question, the AI receptionist either resolves it directly (using a knowledge base you provide) or routes it to the right admin person with context. The goal is to stop these calls from interrupting recruiters.



Technical and integration

Does BlueWave need an IT team to maintain this?

No. BlueWave has no engineering staff and the system is designed with that in mind. The audit interface is CMS-level simple — think WordPress, not a developer console. Routine operations like reviewing agent decisions, flagging errors, and adjusting routing rules don’t require technical skills. Mirror Factory provides ongoing support for anything more complex.

How does Swell connect to our existing systems?

Swell integrates with three systems you already use: Bullhorn (your ATS/CRM — this is the core integration), RingCentral (your phone system — calls route through here), and Slack (for internal notifications). No new software needs to be installed on anyone’s computer. The system runs in the cloud and connects to your existing tools through their APIs.

Is our data safe? Where does it go?

This is a closed-loop deployment. Your candidate data, call recordings, and business information stay within the system — no external data exposure. BlueWave owns all data processed through Swell. Mirror Factory has no data retention rights beyond what’s needed for active development and support. The specific deployment model (cloud vs. local vs. hybrid) will be finalized during Phase 1, with data privacy as a hard requirement.

What if the AI makes a mistake on a call?

The system is designed to start cautiously and expand gradually. During the pilot, Swell begins with low-stakes call types — admin routing, status checks — before handling candidate screening. When the AI encounters something it’s not confident about, it transfers to a human with full context rather than guessing. Every decision is logged and auditable. The weekly review process lets your team catch errors, provide corrections, and improve the system. The AI gets better over time based on your team’s feedback.



Budget and investment

What does this cost?

Phase 1 (Design and Development) is $20,000 fixed — this covers 4–6 weeks of design, prototyping, and building the MVP focused on inbound triage. Phase 2 (Pilot, continued development, and consulting) is estimated at $120,000–$180,000 over approximately 90 days. The total investment is $140,000–$200,000 for a working, validated system.

How does this compare to subscribing to an off-the-shelf tool?

Enterprise AI staffing platforms typically cost $120,000–$360,000+ per year in subscription fees — and that cost recurs every year with no IP ownership. Swell’s total investment reaches cost parity with SaaS alternatives within two years while creating a proprietary software asset BlueWave owns outright. The subscription tools also can’t be customized to BlueWave’s specific workflows, and the capability disappears if you cancel.

When does BlueWave start seeing value?

Phase 1 delivers a working prototype in 4–6 weeks that your team can evaluate hands-on. The pilot (Phase 2) runs for approximately 90 days with the system handling real calls internally. You’ll see measurable impact — calls handled, time saved, screening results — during the pilot, not after it. Each phase has explicit success criteria and a go/no-go decision point, so you’re never committing to the next phase without evidence that the current one worked.

What’s the ongoing cost after the build?

Mirror Factory provides support through a service agreement. The specifics will be defined during Phase 1, but the model is lightweight — CMS-like self-service for routine operations, with Mirror Factory handling complex changes and system improvements. This is significantly less than the initial build cost.



Strategy and ownership

Who owns the software?

BlueWave owns all custom-built software outright — the call routing logic, ticketing system, screening rubrics, Bullhorn and RingCentral integrations, and the messaging bus. Full source code, full documentation, no lock-in. Mirror Factory retains ownership of Layers (our reusable platform infrastructure), which BlueWave receives a perpetual, irrevocable license to use. BlueWave can operate Swell independently of Mirror Factory at any time.

Why build instead of buying an existing product?

Three reasons. First, no existing product does what BlueWave needs — we evaluated RingCentral AIR, Bullhorn Amplify, Tenzo AI, Whippy, ConverzAI, and a dozen other tools. Each covers one piece of the puzzle, but none combine inbound voice triage across multiple caller types with deep Bullhorn integration and outbound screening. Second, every off-the-shelf tool is a subscription — BlueWave owns nothing. Third, owning proprietary software IP is a strategic lever for the business. It’s an asset on the balance sheet, not a line item on a vendor invoice.

What happens if this doesn’t work?

Every phase has a decision gate. Phase 1 ends with a working prototype and defined success criteria for the pilot. Phase 2 ends with a go/no-go decision based on real performance data — calls handled, accuracy, recruiter feedback. If the pilot doesn’t meet the criteria, you don’t proceed to Phase 3. The phased structure means you’re validating with $20,000 before committing $120,000+. Phase 1 is designed as a proof of concept, not a leap of faith.

Can Swell eventually be sold or licensed to other staffing firms?

That’s a possibility worth evaluating during Phase 3 (Expansion). The architecture is designed so that Swell could be packaged for other firms — but the decision on whether to pursue productization vs. keeping it as an internal competitive advantage is BlueWave’s to make. It doesn’t affect how we build Phase 1 or Phase 2.







This document is part of the Swell engagement proposal prepared by Mirror Factory for BlueWave Resource Partners.

Phase 2 ends with a go/no-go decision based on real performance data — calls handled, accuracy, recruiter feedback. If the pilot doesn’t meet the criteria, you don’t proceed to Phase 3. The phased structure means you’re validating with $20,000 before committing $120,000+. Phase 1 is designed as a proof of concept, not a leap of faith.

Can Swell eventually be sold or licensed to other staffing firms?

That’s a possibility worth evaluating during Phase 3 (Expansion). The architecture is designed so that Swell could be packaged for other firms — but the decision on whether to pursue productization vs. keeping it as an internal competitive advantage is BlueWave’s to make. It doesn’t affect how we build Phase 1 or Phase 2.

This document is part of the Swell engagement proposal prepared by Mirror Factory for BlueWave Resource Partners.

Prepared by: Mirror Factory

Prepared for: BlueWave Leadership Team

1. Swell

Mirror Factory proposes a phased engagement to design, build, and pilot Swell: a proprietary AI-powered call triage and candidate intelligence system for BlueWave Resource Partners. Swell answers a simple question: what if every call to BlueWave’s main number was answered instantly, routed intelligently, and produced a contextual record that made the next human interaction better?

Inbound call triage

An AI receptionist answers every call, determines the caller’s intent through natural conversation, and routes them to the right department or agent. No phone trees, no hold music, no wasted time. When a candidate calls, the system transitions seamlessly to a recruiter agent that leads a pre-screening conversation, evaluates the candidate against qualifying criteria, and writes structured data directly to Bullhorn. Every call generates a progressive ticket that fills with context as the conversation unfolds. When a human picks up, they already know what’s going on.



Outbound candidate screening

When BlueWave receives a new job requisition, Swell screens hundreds of candidates simultaneously against the real qualifying criteria and delivers a qualified shortlist within one hour. This compresses what currently takes days of manual sorting through databases, resumes, and LinkedIn profiles into minutes of intelligent, automated screening. The recruiting team gets a Slack notification and a Bullhorn tear sheet, ready to start calling qualified, interested people.



Integration and ownership

Swell integrates directly with BlueWave’s existing systems: Bullhorn for candidate data (200,000+ records), RingCentral for telephony, and Slack for internal notifications. No data leaves your ecosystem. The system is designed for a private, closed-loop deployment. BlueWave owns all custom-built Swell IP outright, including full source code and documentation, with no vendor lock-in. Mirror Factory’s platform tools are licensed to BlueWave with a perpetual, irrevocable license.

2. Goals and Success Criteria

These are the measurable outcomes that define success for this engagement.

Eliminate low-value inbound call handling. Calls to BlueWave’s main number are triaged by an AI receptionist and routed to the right person or resolved without human intervention. Self-service tasks like ADP password resets and status checks are handled by the agent directly.

Pre-screen candidates before human follow-up. A recruiter agent captures structured evaluation data (3 to 5 qualifying criteria, availability, compensation expectations) and writes it to Bullhorn, so human recruiters start every conversation with context instead of a cold call.

Compress time-to-qualified-shortlist. Reduce the cycle from receiving a new job requisition to having a contacted, qualified candidate shortlist from days of manual sorting to under one hour through automated outbound screening.

Build a native ticketing and context system. Every call generates a progressive ticket with contextual summaries tailored to the recipient. Repeat callers are automatically merged. Warm handoffs happen with full context, not a transcript dump.

Establish BlueWave-owned software IP. Swell is architected as a separable asset owned entirely by BlueWave, with no vendor lock-in, increasing company value as a standalone technology asset.

Validate through a controlled pilot. A 90-day internal pilot with progressive delegation: start with low-stakes call types, expand as confidence builds, and end with a clear go/no-go decision for broader deployment.

3. The Business Case

Three value dimensions justify this investment: operational efficiency, revenue capacity, and competitive positioning.

Recruiter productivity

BlueWave’s 18 to 20 recruiters currently spend 75 to 80% of their time on unqualified candidates. At a conservative fully-loaded cost of $50,000 per recruiter, that represents $675,000 to $750,000 per year in labor allocated to low-yield screening. Industry benchmarks confirm this pattern: staffing agencies using AI report 23% higher placement rates alongside lower costs, and AI screening cuts time-to-shortlist by 75%. Swell is designed to invert this ratio so recruiters spend the majority of their time on qualified, interested candidates rather than sorting through databases.

Revenue capacity without headcount growth

BlueWave’s team is at capacity. Growing placement volume today requires hiring more recruiters. Swell changes that equation. If each recruiter can intelligently manage 5,000 to 10,000 candidates instead of approximately 1,000, the team’s capacity to handle requisitions grows without proportional headcount. Staffing firms using AI voice screening report 10x candidate reach and 60% reduction in recruiter phone time without adding headcount. Industry data shows that automation saving each recruiter four hours per week recovers over $5,000 in productive capacity every month for a team of eight recruiters. BlueWave has more than twice that many. Even a conservative 2x increase in placement capacity at BlueWave’s revenue scale represents significant incremental revenue without additional salary expense.

Competitive positioning

BlueWave evaluated RingCentral AIR, Bullhorn Amplify, Tenzo AI, and several third-party staffing tools. None solve the full problem: multi-caller-type inbound triage with deep Bullhorn integration, combined with outbound screening at scale. The market gap validates the custom-build approach. The staffing agencies that thrive in 2026 will not be the ones with the most recruiters, but the ones whose recruiters are augmented by AI that handles screening, scheduling, and outreach, freeing humans to focus on relationships and closing.

Building Swell as proprietary software rather than subscribing to a third-party platform means BlueWave owns the asset. It appears on the balance sheet, it can be demonstrated to potential acquirers, and it can be evaluated for licensing to other staffing firms. No existing product handles this full problem, which means Swell has no direct off-the-shelf equivalent.

What comparable tools cost

For context, comparable SaaS solutions in the staffing vertical command significant annual investment. Medium-tier recruiting platforms cost $100 to $200 per user per month, and enterprise-grade AI platforms with custom integrations range from $25,000 to $150,000 in implementation costs alone in year one, plus ongoing subscription fees. BlueWave’s investment in Swell produces a system it owns outright: no recurring subscription that appreciates for someone else, no data locked in a vendor’s ecosystem, and no dependency on a third party’s product roadmap.

4. How Swell Works

This section describes the system’s inner workings: the AI agents that power it, the voice experience callers interact with, the ticketing system that captures context, and the tools your team uses to audit and improve it over time.

4.1 AI agent architecture

Swell is powered by three AI agents, each with a single, clear responsibility.

Receptionist agent. Answers every inbound call with a branded introduction, gathers intent through open-ended conversation, and routes the caller to the correct path: Sales, Admin/HR, or Candidates. The Receptionist can also resolve simple requests directly, like providing ADP password reset instructions or answering benefits questions from a knowledge base. When the Receptionist encounters uncertainty, the default is to escalate to a human with full context, not to guess.

Recruiter agent. Activates when the Receptionist identifies a candidate. The Recruiter leads a structured pre-screening conversation: it drives the discussion, evaluates the candidate against 3 to 5 qualifying criteria defined per job type, captures structured output (candidate profile, evaluation scores, call summary), and writes everything to Bullhorn. The candidate experiences a seamless conversation, not a transfer. Context gathered by the Receptionist is passed forward so screening starts with information, not from scratch.

Screening agent (outbound). Triggered when a new job requisition enters Bullhorn. The Screening Agent ingests the job description, extracts the real qualifying criteria (stripping out boilerplate), searches BlueWave’s 200,000+ candidate database, and initiates simultaneous outreach via phone, text, and email. Candidate responses are evaluated against the screening rubric, scored, and compiled into a qualified shortlist pushed to a Bullhorn tear sheet.

4.2 Voice experience

The external voice experience is the primary interface for all inbound callers. It must feel natural, professional, and human-like. Callers should not feel like they are navigating a phone tree or talking to a robot. Conversations are open-ended and adaptive: the agent handles natural patterns like interruptions, corrections, pauses, and topic changes.

The voice pipeline targets under 800 milliseconds of total latency (speech-to-text, LLM inference, and text-to-speech combined) to maintain a natural conversational feel. Voice model selection (cloud services like ElevenLabs, AWS Bedrock, or local models like Gemma 26B+) will be resolved during Phase 1 based on security requirements, latency testing, and cost analysis.

4.3 Progressive ticketing system

Every inbound call instantly creates a blank ticket that fills progressively as the conversation unfolds. This is not a raw transcript. It is a structured, contextual record that includes caller ID, intent classification, routing decision, conversation summary, evaluation data (if applicable), resolution status, and a full action trace of every agent decision.

When the same person calls again, tickets are automatically merged by phone number, name match, or Bullhorn candidate ID. Prior context surfaces immediately: the agent knows this is Fraser’s sixth call about the Disney electrical engineer position. When a call routes to a human, the ticket provides a summary tailored to the recipient: recruiters get candidate-fit summaries, admin gets resolution context, and leadership gets escalation context.

4.4 Internal messaging bus

A central routing layer delivers agent notifications, ticket summaries, and escalation alerts to the right person in the right channel. The system routes across text (SMS), Slack, and email based on BlueWave’s internal SOPs: who gets what message type, department-specific rules, and urgency tiers. Routing rules are configurable without code changes. Each message includes contextual information from the relevant ticket, and recipients can act directly from the notification (for example, clicking through to the relevant Bullhorn record).

4.5 Audit and feedback interface

Every agent action is logged and traceable. The BlueWave team can review agent behavior through a CMS-level simple interface: review the past week’s interactions, see the full decision path for any call, flag incorrect actions, and provide corrections that are incorporated into the agent’s context. The system tracks correction history to identify recurring error patterns. Think of it like managing a team of interns: clear instructions, controlled environment, regular audits, and continuous improvement through feedback loops.

5. Engagement Plan

The engagement is structured in three phases. The first two are priced in this proposal. The third is outlined for planning purposes and will be scoped based on Phase 2 outcomes.

Phase 1: Design, discovery, and prototyping (4 to 6 weeks)

Mirror Factory embeds into BlueWave’s operational environment. We observe real workflows, work with real Bullhorn data, and iterate rapidly through design and prototyping cycles. This follows a human-centered design process: understand your team’s daily reality through direct observation and collaboration, define routing and screening logic through iterative design sessions with key stakeholders, prototype working agent systems against real call scenarios, and validate through hands-on testing.

Immersive discovery and requirements gathering. Embed with BlueWave’s team to observe and map call routing logic across Sales, Admin/HR, and Candidate paths. Document messaging SOPs and escalation rules through direct collaboration. Confirm Bullhorn and RingCentral API capabilities (read/write on candidate records, tear sheets, job requisitions).

Agent architecture and prototyping. Design and build the AI receptionist agent (intent gathering, routing, self-service resolution) and recruiter agent (pre-screening conversation, rubric evaluation, structured output). Establish agent context profiles, guardrails (what agents should never do), and escalation rules.

Integration development. Connect to Bullhorn for reading and writing candidate records, tear sheets, and job requisitions. Connect to RingCentral for inbound call routing to the AI receptionist. Set up Slack notifications for the internal messaging bus.

Ticketing system design and build. Build the native ticketing system: progressive ticket creation on call connect, automatic repeat caller merging, and contextual handoff summaries tailored per recipient role.

QA plan and pilot preparation. Define the testing strategy (human callers, TTS simulation, or hybrid), establish success metrics and KPIs, and define Phase 2 milestones. Prepare the system for internal pilot deployment. Resolve deployment model (cloud, local, or hybrid) based on security and cost evaluation.

Phase 2: Development, pilot, and consulting (approximately 90 days)

Continued development of both inbound and outbound systems alongside a live internal pilot. Mirror Factory provides weekly consulting throughout, with monthly formal reviews on agent performance, error rates, and system improvements.

Internal pilot deployment. Launch inbound triage for live calls with progressive delegation. Start with low-stakes call types (Admin/HR routing, self-service requests) before expanding to candidate pre-screening. Monthly goals and expansion gates.

Continued inbound development and QA. Refine agent behavior based on real-world performance data. Execute the QA plan using the hybrid approach defined in Phase 1. Test edge cases: unclear intent, caller frustration, immediate human requests, repeat callers, background noise, speakerphone. Validate voice quality across real-world conditions.

Outbound screening development. Architecture and development of the outbound candidate screening system: requisition ingestion, criteria extraction, Bullhorn database matching, simultaneous candidate outreach, and shortlist compilation. Designed in parallel with the inbound pilot.

Consulting, support, and team training. Weekly reviews with the BlueWave team. Agent performance reporting and error flagging. Training your team on the audit interface and feedback workflow. Ongoing iteration on agent context, routing rules, and screening rubrics based on team input.

90-day pilot review. Formal review at the conclusion of the pilot covering agent performance against baseline metrics, corrections log analysis, system reliability, and recommendations for Phase 3. This review is the go/no-go gate for expansion.

Phase 3: Expansion and productization (future, not priced)

This is where Swell evolves from an internal tool into a scalable product and BlueWave’s competitive advantage compounds.

Activate outbound candidate screening. Full deployment of the screening system. The system contacts hundreds of candidates simultaneously against a new job requisition and delivers a qualified, interested shortlist within one hour. Cost controls established for per-click sourcing tools (Indeed, Dice, ZipRecruiter) before enabling agent access.

Evaluate productization. Assess packaging Swell 1.0 for licensing to other staffing firms. Architecture decisions around multi-tenancy, documentation standards, and portability made based on Phase 2 learnings.

Design additional AI tools. Sales workflow automation, compliance tools, outbound business development, and shared inbox management. These are designed based on what your team surfaces during the Phase 2 pilot.

Implement system learning. Agents improve automatically over time by learning from placement outcomes, response rates, screening question effectiveness, and recruiter feedback. Learning insights are surfaced in the audit interface for review.

Expand channel coverage. Website chatbot integration, additional communication channels, and deeper workflow automation across BlueWave’s operations.

6. Testing and Quality Assurance

Voice-based AI systems present unique QA challenges. Unlike text-based software where automated testing can cover thousands of scenarios, voice requires either real human interaction or high-quality speech simulation. The external voice experience is the highest-risk surface: caller trust depends entirely on voice quality and conversational naturalness.

QA approach

We recommend a hybrid approach combining two methods. The exact mix depends on budget, which is a design input resolved during Phase 1.

Hired human callers. External callers run scripted and adversarial scenarios against the system. Highest fidelity testing. Covers real-world variability: accents, background noise, interruptions, edge cases.

TTS scripted simulation. Write call scripts and use text-to-speech to simulate callers at scale. Higher coverage than internal testing, lower fidelity than real humans. Effective for regression testing and known-scenario validation.

Both approaches are viable. A higher QA budget enables a robust human caller program plus TTS automation for regression, targeting 100+ scripted test calls before live deployment. A leaner budget leans TTS-heavy with targeted human testing for critical paths.

Pre-launch testing requirements

Before any live deployment, the following must be validated:

All three routing paths (Sales, Admin/HR, Candidates) tested end-to-end.

Recruiter agent screening validated against at least 3 job types.

Edge cases tested: unclear intent, caller frustration, immediate human request, repeat callers, wrong numbers.

Ticketing validated: creation, progressive fill, merging, and contextual summaries.

Messaging bus validated: correct channel, correct person, correct urgency tier.

Voice quality validated across environments: mobile, landline, speakerphone, background noise.

Success metrics

Formal success metrics will be defined during Phase 1 and serve as the evaluation criteria for the Phase 2 go/no-go gate. Candidate metrics include:

Call resolution rate: percentage of calls resolved without human escalation.

Time-to-qualified-shortlist: hours from requisition receipt to shortlist delivery (outbound screening).

Recruiter hours saved per week.

Ticket accuracy: percentage rated useful by the receiving team member.

Error rate: percentage of calls requiring post-hoc correction.

Candidate satisfaction signal: qualitative feedback from the recruiting team.

9. About Mirror Factory

Mirror Factory is an AI-native design and development studio based in Orlando, specializing in building proprietary AI systems for organizations that want to own their technology, not subscribe to someone else’s. We focus on context management: helping teams design, build, and maintain the information infrastructure that makes AI agents effective, trustworthy, and improvable over time.

Our team brings over a decade of experience in software development, spanning virtual reality simulation, real-time systems, creative technology, and AI product design. We operate as a hybrid of consulting and platform: we design and build custom solutions while contributing reusable patterns and tools to our own platform, which benefits every client engagement.

For BlueWave, this means you get a team that understands both the technology and the operational context. We are not deploying a generic SaaS tool. We are embedding with your team, learning your workflows, and building a system that is yours.
